text: Elite junior cross-country skiers distribute approximately 75% of endurance training sessions in zone 1 (below VT1), 6-8% in zone 2 (between VT1 and VT2), and 15-20% in zone 3 (above VT2).
Heart rate-based and session RPE-based methods produce similar training intensity distributions, validating session RPE as a practical alternative to continuous heart rate monitoring.
Elite endurance athletes train surprisingly little at lactate threshold intensity, with only 6-8% of sessions falling in the zone between VT1 and VT2.
A polarized training distribution with most sessions clearly below or substantially above the lactate accommodation zone is consistently observed across multiple elite endurance sports.
Blood lactate measurements confirm the polarized pattern: 71% of sessions were performed at ≤2.0 mM, 7% between 2-4 mM, and 22% above 4 mM (mean 9.5 mM for high-intensity sessions).
Training intensity zones should be delineated using ventilatory thresholds (VT1 and VT2) to create a three-zone model that distinguishes easy, threshold, and high-intensity work.
